pages.title: Welcome to Vendor Invoice and Email Fraud
pages.subtitle: Learn how fake emails, impersonation, and payment scams can trick businesses into sending money to the wrong place.

video.caption: Introduction to vendor invoice and email fraud

pages.title: Why this matters
pages.text:
Vendor invoice fraud is not just an accounting problem. It is often a cybersecurity issue because attackers use email compromise, fake domains, impersonation, or stolen accounts to make a payment request look real.

A criminal may pretend to be a trusted vendor, coworker, manager, or executive. They may ask for updated banking details, a rushed payment, or a wire transfer.

If someone acts without verifying the request, the organization could lose money, expose sensitive information, or create a larger security incident.

pages.title: How this fraud usually works
pages.text:
Most vendor payment scams follow a similar pattern:

  1. A scammer sends a message that appears to come from a real vendor or internal employee.
  2. The message asks for a payment, invoice approval, or bank account change.
  3. The request is made to look routine, urgent, or confidential.
  4. If no one verifies it, payment may be sent to the attacker instead of the real vendor.

Sometimes the attacker uses a look-alike email address. In other cases, they may have access to a real account and continue an existing email conversation.

Sample suspicious email patterns:

ExampleWhat is wrong
From: billing@acme-suppl1es.com
Subject: Updated banking details for future payments
Message: Please use our new account information for this month’s invoice.		The sender address is look-alike and the message is asking for a banking change by email.
From: accounts@realvendor.com
Subject: Urgent: Pay today to avoid service interruption
Message: We need immediate payment today. Do not delay.		The message uses urgency to push quick action before verification.
From: ceo.company.request@gmail.com
Subject: Need you to process this wire now
Message: I am in a meeting. Handle this quietly and send confirmation once done.		It uses a non-company email address, secrecy, and pressure to bypass normal approval steps.

These examples are for training only. Do not trust a payment request just because it looks familiar.

pages.title: Real-life incident example
pages.text:
Vendor and payment fraud are not just training topics. Real organizations have lost money because a request looked routine enough to trust.

This short news clip gives a real-world example of how financial fraud can affect an organization and why strong verification steps matter.

Use this example to reinforce one key point: even familiar processes like invoices, approvals, or vendor payments can be abused when people rely on appearance instead of verification.

This video is included for educational reference only. We are not affiliated with WKYC.

video.caption: Real-life news example related to financial fraud

pages.title: Red flags to watch for
pages.text:
Be cautious whenever a message involves money, account changes, or urgency.

  • New banking instructions sent by email only
  • Pressure to act quickly or keep the request secret
  • An email address that looks slightly different from the real vendor
  • A sudden change in tone, wording, or formatting
  • Unexpected attachments, invoices, or payment requests
  • A request that bypasses normal approval steps

Even if the message looks familiar, verify it before processing payment.

pages.title: What you should do
pages.text:
If you receive a message about invoices, payment changes, or urgent transfers:

  1. Pause before acting.
  2. Do not rely on email alone to confirm payment changes.
  3. Verify the request using a trusted method, such as a known phone number or approved vendor contact.
  4. Report suspicious messages to your manager, finance lead, IT, or security contact.
  5. Do not send payment until the request has been confirmed.

A short verification step can prevent a major financial loss.

pages.title: Did you know?
pages.text:
• Finance, payroll, purchasing, and operations staff are common targets because they regularly handle payments and vendor communication.
  • A familiar display name is not enough to prove an email is legitimate.
  • Urgency is a common tactic because attackers want people to act before they verify.
  • These scams do not always use malware. Many succeed by manipulating trust and routine business processes.
